content: Accelerated approval is an FDA pathway allowing earlier approval of drugs for serious or life-threatening conditions based on surrogate endpoints reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit, rather than direct evidence of clinical benefit. This pathway was established in 1992 primarily for HIV/AIDS treatments and is now used extensively in oncology, rare diseases, and other areas. Drugs approved via accelerated approval require post-marketing confirmatory trials to verify the anticipated clinical benefit. If confirmatory trials fail to verify benefit, FDA can withdraw approval.
